levers.name: MitID Ecosystem Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging the MitID ecosystem will provide valuable insights into their current trust model, identify potential points of intervention, and help shape their future development. However, this may also lead to pushback from existing operators who are invested in maintaining the status quo.
options: Deepen collaboration with MitID stakeholders to inform AltID requirements
Develop a parallel identity platform that competes directly with MitID
Engage with MitID developers to incorporate open standards and fallback capabilities into their architecture
levers.review_lever: The project assumes that existing operators will be receptive to the idea of incorporating fallback capabilities and open standards, but in reality, they may resist changes that threaten their market share. This lever misses the critical aspect of addressing incumbent resistance and regulatory friction.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to engage with existing identity infrastructure

levers.name: Standards-based Fallback Demonstrators
levers.consequences: Developing demonstrators that showcase standards-based fallback capabilities will provide critical technical evidence to inform procurement decisions and influence AltID requirements. However, this may also highlight the limitations of current open standards in meeting Denmark's specific digital identity needs.
options: Create a mobile-oriented demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect
Develop a browser-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on mobile OS duopoly
Explore hardware-token-based fallback options for critical public authentication
levers.review_lever: The project's focus on standards-based fallback demonstrators assumes that existing open standards will be sufficient to meet Denmark's digital identity needs, but in reality, the project may need to consider custom or hybrid solutions to address specific requirements.
levers.lever_classification: product — what technology to use for fallback authentication

levers.name: Procurement Language and Standards Pressure
levers.consequences: Influencing procurement language and standards pressure will help shape AltID requirements and ensure that Danish digital identity systems are designed with platform-neutrality and fallback capabilities in mind. However, this may also lead to resistance from existing operators who are invested in maintaining the status quo.
options: Secure inclusion of platform-neutrality and fallback access language in official design documents
Engage with EU bodies and technical working groups to align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements
Develop procurement language that prioritizes supplier-neutral design and certified fallback capabilities
levers.review_lever: The project assumes that existing operators will be receptive to the idea of incorporating platform-neutrality and fallback capabilities into AltID requirements, but in reality, they may resist changes that threaten their market share.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to influence procurement decisions

levers.name: Resilience Framing and Digital Sovereignty
levers.consequences: Framing the issue of digital identity in terms of resilience, continuity of service, and supplier concentration risk will help shift the narrative away from convenience arguments and towards a focus on digital sovereignty. However, this may also lead to pushback from existing operators who are invested in maintaining the status quo.
options: Reframe the issue as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration and reduced strategic autonomy
Develop a resilience-focused communication strategy to engage stakeholders and influence AltID requirements
Engage with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups to align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements
levers.review_lever: The project assumes that existing operators will be receptive to the idea of prioritizing digital sovereignty over convenience, but in reality, they may resist changes that threaten their market share.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to frame the issue

levers.name: EU-level Engagement and Alignment
levers.consequences: Engaging with EU bodies and technical working groups will help align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. However, this may also lead to delays or resistance from existing operators who are invested in maintaining the status quo.
options: Secure inclusion of platform-neutral access language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials
Engage with EU bodies and technical working groups to influence procurement decisions and standards pressure
Develop a strategy for aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements
levers.review_lever: The project assumes that existing operators will be receptive to the idea of prioritizing platform-neutrality and fallback capabilities, but in reality, they may resist changes that threaten their market share.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to engage with EU bodies

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Fallback Governance
levers.consequences: Establishing platform-neutral fallback governance would ensure continuity of service in case of platform vendor changes or disruptions, but it might also lead to increased regulatory complexity and costs. One potential trade-off is that the more robust fallback system could be perceived as less secure than a single-platform solution.
options: Implement a decentralized architecture with multiple identity providers
Develop an open-source fallback authentication protocol
Create a publicly-funded entity to oversee platform-neutral identity services
levers.review_lever: The options miss the complexity of implementing and maintaining a decentralized architecture, which could lead to issues with scalability, interoperability, and user adoption.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to ensure continuity of service in case of platform vendor changes or disruptions

levers.name: Resilience-Based Procurement Language
levers.consequences: Including resilience-based procurement language would emphasize the need for a robust fallback system, but it might also lead to increased costs and complexity in procurement processes. One potential trade-off is that the focus on resilience could lead to a lack of consideration for other important factors such as user experience and security.
options: Emphasize the importance of a robust fallback system in procurement documents
Develop specific requirements for platform-neutral authentication protocols
Create a scoring system to evaluate vendors' resilience capabilities
levers.review_lever: The options do not account for the potential trade-offs between emphasizing resilience and other important factors, which could lead to an imbalanced procurement process.
levers.lever_classification: procurement — how to ensure procurement processes prioritize resilience

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement and Standards Pressure
levers.consequences: Engaging with EU bodies and applying standards pressure would help align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements, but it might also lead to increased complexity and costs due to the need for harmonization. One potential trade-off is that the focus on EU-level engagement could lead to a lack of consideration for national specificities.
options: Engage with relevant EU bodies to develop platform-neutral standards
Apply pressure through technical working groups to include resilience requirements
Create a joint statement with other European countries to emphasize the importance of platform-neutral identity services
levers.review_lever: The options do not account for the potential trade-offs between emphasizing EU-level engagement and national specificities, which could lead to an imbalanced approach.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to ensure Danish advocacy aligns with broader European requirements

levers.name: Digital Sovereignty Framing
levers.consequences: Framing the issue in terms of digital sovereignty would emphasize the importance of control and autonomy, but it might also lead to increased polarization and conflict. One potential trade-off is that the focus on digital sovereignty could lead to a lack of consideration for other important factors such as user experience and security.
options: Emphasize the need for Denmark to maintain control over its digital identity infrastructure
Develop specific requirements for platform-neutral authentication protocols based on digital sovereignty principles
Create a public awareness campaign to emphasize the importance of digital sovereignty
levers.review_lever: The options do not account for the potential trade-offs between emphasizing digital sovereignty and other important factors, which could lead to an imbalanced approach.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to ensure Denmark maintains control over its digital identity infrastructure

levers.name: Resilience-Focused Demonstrator Development
levers.consequences: Developing resilience-focused demonstrators would provide credible technical evidence for a robust fallback system, but it might also lead to increased costs and complexity in demonstrator development. One potential trade-off is that the focus on resilience could lead to a lack of consideration for other important factors such as user experience and security.
options: Develop a demonstrator that showcases a decentralized architecture
Create a demonstrator that highlights the importance of platform-neutral authentication protocols
Build a demonstrator that emphasizes the need for a robust fallback system
levers.review_lever: The options do not account for the potential trade-offs between emphasizing resilience and other important factors, which could lead to an imbalanced approach.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to develop credible technical evidence for a robust fallback system

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Standards Development
levers.consequences: Developing platform-neutral standards would provide a clear framework for vendors, but it might also lead to increased complexity and costs due to the need for harmonization. One potential trade-off is that the focus on standards development could lead to a lack of consideration for national specificities.
options: Engage with relevant EU bodies to develop platform-neutral standards
Develop specific requirements for platform-neutral authentication protocols based on digital sovereignty principles
Create a publicly-funded entity to oversee platform-neutral identity services
levers.review_lever: The options do not account for the potential trade-offs between emphasizing standards development and national specificities, which could lead to an imbalanced approach.
levers.lever_classification: standards — how to ensure Denmark develops platform-neutral standards

levers.name: MitID Ecosystem Standards Advocacy
levers.consequences: Advocating for open standards in the MitID ecosystem can lead to a more secure and resilient digital identity infrastructure, but it may also create friction with existing platform providers who have invested heavily in proprietary solutions.
options: Influence industry associations to adopt FIDO2 and OpenID Connect as de facto standards
Develop a roadmap for transitioning MitID clients to open standards-based authentication flows
Pilot a proof-of-concept implementation of open standards within the existing MitID framework
levers.review_lever: While advocating for open standards is crucial, the project should also consider the potential costs and challenges associated with changing the existing ecosystem. A weakness in the options provided is that they do not account for the possibility that some stakeholders may resist or even actively work against the adoption of open standards.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which data collection approach to use

levers.name: Digital Identity Standards Harmonization
levers.consequences: Harmonizing digital identity standards across Denmark can lead to increased interoperability and reduced fragmentation, but it may also require significant investment in updating existing infrastructure and processes.
options: Develop a national digital identity standards framework that aligns with European eIDAS regulations
Establish a working group to harmonize digital identity standards across government agencies and private sector stakeholders
Conduct a gap analysis to identify areas where existing digital identity standards can be improved or updated
levers.review_lever: While harmonizing digital identity standards is essential, the project should also consider the potential costs and challenges associated with changing existing processes and infrastructure. A weakness in the options provided is that they do not account for the possibility that some stakeholders may resist or even actively work against the adoption of new standards.
levers.lever_classification: standards — what data to standardize

levers.name: Procurement Language and EU-level Engagement
levers.consequences: Influencing procurement language and engaging with EU bodies can lead to increased platform neutrality and reduced dependence on foreign technology suppliers, but it may also require significant effort and resources to build a coalition of support.
options: Develop a set of procurement guidelines that prioritize open standards and platform neutrality
Engage with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups to influence the development of EU-level digital identity standards
Build a coalition of civil society organizations, industry associations, and government agencies to advocate for platform-neutral procurement practices
levers.review_lever: While influencing procurement language and engaging with EU bodies is crucial, the project should also consider the potential costs and challenges associated with building a coalition of support. A weakness in the options provided is that they do not account for the possibility that some stakeholders may resist or even actively work against the adoption of platform-neutral procurement practices.
levers.lever_classification: procurement — what to procure

levers.name: Digital Sovereignty Framing and Advocacy
levers.consequences: Framing digital identity as a matter of national sovereignty can lead to increased government interest and support, but it may also create friction with existing stakeholders who have invested heavily in proprietary solutions.
options: Develop a narrative that positions digital identity as a key component of Denmark's national sovereignty
Engage with government agencies and industry associations to build support for platform-neutral digital identity standards
Conduct a public awareness campaign to educate citizens about the importance of digital sovereignty in digital identity
levers.review_lever: While framing digital identity as a matter of national sovereignty is essential, the project should also consider the potential costs and challenges associated with changing existing stakeholder perceptions. A weakness in the options provided is that they do not account for the possibility that some stakeholders may resist or even actively work against the adoption of platform-neutral digital identity standards.
levers.lever_classification: governance — what to govern

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Fallback Governance
levers.consequences: Establishing a governance framework for platform-neutral fallback authentication can lead to increased resilience and reduced dependence on foreign technology suppliers, but it may also require significant effort and resources to build a coalition of support.
options: Develop a set of governance guidelines that prioritize platform neutrality and fallback authentication
Engage with industry associations and government agencies to build support for platform-neutral fallback authentication standards
Conduct a public awareness campaign to educate citizens about the importance of platform-neutral fallback authentication in digital identity
levers.review_lever: While establishing a governance framework for platform-neutral fallback authentication is crucial, the project should also consider the potential costs and challenges associated with building a coalition of support. A weakness in the options provided is that they do not account for the possibility that some stakeholders may resist or even actively work against the adoption of platform-neutral fallback authentication standards.
levers.lever_classification: governance — who oversees the review process

Build a coalition of civil society organizations, industry associations, and government agencies to advocate for platform-neutral digital identity standards

levers.lever_classification: standards — what data to standardize

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

2026-Mar-08
